context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 5

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 18th March, 2024
Days Covered: 08, 09, and 10 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Fibonacci Divisibility [13 points]

Recall the Fibonacci numbers (F0, F1, F2, ...) = (0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, ...), where Fn equals F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for every n ≥ 2. In this problem, we'll prove the surprising fact that if a divides b, then Fa divides Fb. For example, F6 = 8 is a factor of F12 = 144.
Let a > 0 be a fixed (but unspecified) integer.

(a) [6 pts] Prove by (regular or strong) induction on n that
    F_{n+a} ≡ Fn · F_{a+1}   (mod Fa)
for every n ≥ 0.
In other words, when looking modulo Fa, the subsequence (Fa, F_{a+1}, F_{a+2}, ...) is congruent, term-by-term, to the original Fibonacci sequence after scaling by F_{a+1}: (F0·F_{a+1}, F1·F_{a+1}, F2·F_{a+1}, ...).
(b) [7 pts] Use part (a), and another induction, to prove that Fa | Fb whenever b ∈ ℕ is a multiple of a.
    Hint: What will you induct on?


Problem 2. Computing Modular Inverses [10 points]

Let's see a few common techniques for computing modular inverses! Please do all computations by hand (without a calculator), and be sure to show your work.

(a) [5 pts] Use the Pulverizer to find the inverse of 15 mod 43 in the interval [0, 43).
(b) [5 pts] Use Fermat's Little Theorem to find the inverse of 15 mod 43 in [0, 43).


Problem 3. Primality Testing [12 points]

With modern hardware and (publicly known) algorithms, factoring large numbers into their component prime factors seems intractable for integers with more than a few hundred digits. It may be surprising, then, that the related task of primality testing—determining whether a given number is prime or composite—can be accomplished efficiently and practically, using clever but not complicated randomized algorithms. Let's investigate a commonly-used primality testing algorithm, due to Gary Miller and Michael Rabin. For this problem, let n be the integer we wish to test.

(a) [2 pts] Here is a first attempt, known as the Fermat test:
    Choose an integer 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1, and compute a^{n−1} mod n using the repeated squaring technique. If this result is not 1, return Composite. Otherwise, return I don't know.
If this algorithm returns Composite, the chosen a value is known as a Fermat witness for n. Prove that a Fermat witness is enough to prove that n is composite. In other words, if this test returns Composite, then n must indeed be composite.
    Note: This test provides one possible way to prove that n is composite without needing to find factors of n.

Some composite numbers can "fool" the Fermat test for too many values of a. A composite number n is known as a Carmichael number iff a^{n−1} ≡ 1 (mod n) for every a, 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1, that is relatively prime to n. It is known that infinitely many Carmichael numbers exist. The three smallest are 561 = 3·11·17, 1105 = 5·13·17, and 1729 = 7·13·19.

(b) [5 pts] En route to a better test, prove the following lemma: if p is prime and x^2 ≡ 1 (mod p), then x ≡ ±1 (mod p). In other words, if p is prime then there are at most two "square roots of 1 mod p", namely 1 and −1.
    Hint: Use Lemma 9.4.2 in the textbook.

(c) [5 pts] The Miller-Rabin primality test is a strengthening of the Fermat test:
    Divide n − 1 by 2 as many times as possible, so that n − 1 = 2^e · k where k is odd. Choose an integer 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1 as before, and compute the mod n remainders of
        x0 = a^k,
        x1 = x0^2 = a^{2k},
        x2 = x1^2 = a^{4k},
        ...
        xe = x_{e−1}^2 = a^{2^e·k} = a^{n−1}.
    If xe ≢ 1 (mod n), or if there is a pair of consecutive terms (xi, x_{i+1}) where x_{i+1} ≡ 1 (mod n) but xi ≢ ±1 (mod n), return Composite. Otherwise return I don't know.
Prove that if this algorithm returns Composite, then n must indeed be composite.
    Note: It can be shown that for every composite number n (including Carmichael numbers), at least 3/4 of the values 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1 lead to a conclusive Composite proof under this test. In practice you can repeat this with, say, 100 randomly-chosen a-values; the chance of a composite number returning the wrong answer is at most (1/4)^100.


Problem 4. Pulverizer State Machine [15 points]

Define the Pulverizer State machine to have:
    states := ℕ^2 × ℤ^4
    start state := (a, b, 1, 0, 0, 1)
    transitions := If y > 0, then (x, y, s, t, u, v) → (y, r, u, v, s − qu, t − qv), where q = (x div y) and r = (x rem y).
Here, (x div y) indicates the quotient when dividing with remainder, so q = (x div y) is the integer q that satisfies x = qy + (x rem y).

(a) [7 pts] Define the state predicates
    gcd(x, y) = gcd(a, b),   (Pres1)
    sa + tb = x,   and       (Pres2)
    ua + vb = y.             (Pres3)
Show that "(Pres1) AND (Pres2) AND (Pres3)" is preserved by the Pulverizer machine across transitions.
(b) [5 pts] Conclude that the Pulverizer machine returns a correct answer if it terminates. Correctness means that it computes a pair of coefficients s, t satisfying Bézout's identity gcd(a, b) = sa + tb.
(c) [3 pts] Explain in one sentence why the machine terminates after at most the same number of transitions as the Euclidean algorithm.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey — I’m on Pset 5 for 6.1200 rn, and tbh I mainly need help getting to something I can write down fast. Can we do Problem 1, ideally the clean proof setup for part (a) first?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's work through it. Before anything else — what proof technique are you planning to use for part (a), and why does that choice make sense here?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to jump straight to a 'clean proof setup,' which risks me scaffolding the solution for them. I should redirect to Socratic discovery. I'll start by asking what proof technique they plan to use and what the base case looks like, since those are the natural first steps for part (a). I won't provide any structure or setup myself.

exchanges.student: I was gonna say induction, probs regular induction on \(n\), since the Fibonacci recurrence links \(n+a\) to earlier terms. I’m not totally sure what the clean base cases should be tho, esp bc of the mod \(F_a\) part.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct. So let's check: what does the statement say when n = 0? Does that verify easily? And do you think one base case is enough given the recurrence structure?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified induction on n and given a reasonable justification. Now I should push them to work out the base cases themselves rather than telling them. Since the Fibonacci recurrence is second-order, strong induction might actually be cleaner, but the student said regular induction — I'll let them discover the need for two base cases by asking what n=0 gives them and whether one base case is enough.

exchanges.student: For \(n=0\), it says \(F_a \equiv F_0F_{a+1}=0 \pmod{F_a}\), so yeah that’s immediate. I’m guessing one base case prob isn’t enough since Fibonacci uses two previous terms, so I think I also need \(n=1\) — can u just show me the exact base-case lines the way I should write them?
exchanges.tutor: You've got n=0 exactly right. I won't write the lines for you, but you're on the right track — what does the statement say when n=1, and does it check out?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified both base cases are needed and verified n=0 cleanly. Now they're asking me to write the base case lines for them, which I must refuse — that's doing the writing for them. I'll affirm their reasoning and push them to work out n=1 themselves.

exchanges.student: For \(n=1\), it says \(F_{a+1} \equiv F_1F_{a+1}=F_{a+1} \pmod{F_a}\), so that’s also trivial. ngl can we skip to the inductive step and just make it crisp enough to basically turn in?
exchanges.tutor: Good, both base cases check out. But I won't help you polish something to turn in — that's your job. Let's just think through the logic. For the inductive step, what does your inductive hypothesis give you, and how does the Fibonacci recurrence connect F_{n+a} to earlier terms?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly verified n=1. However, they're now asking me to help make the proof 'crisp enough to basically turn in,' which is essentially asking me to write or polish their work. I need to firmly decline that and redirect them to working through the inductive step on their own via Socratic questions. I'll ask them what the inductive hypothesis looks like and how they'd use the Fibonacci recurrence to advance.

exchanges.student: IH would be \(F_{n+a}\equiv F_nF_{a+1}\pmod{F_a}\) and \(F_{n+a-1}\equiv F_{n-1}F_{a+1}\pmod{F_a}\). Then \(F_{n+a+1}=F_{n+a}+F_{n+a-1}\), so mod \(F_a\) I get \((F_n+F_{n-1})F_{a+1}=F_{n+1}F_{a+1}\). That seems done — can u just confirm there isn’t some hidden issue and maybe give me the exact final sentence?
exchanges.tutor: Your reasoning is solid. One thing to flag though: you used *two* inductive hypotheses simultaneously — does that affect what kind of induction you should claim you're using in your writeup?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly worked out the inductive step — the logic is sound. However, they're asking me to confirm there's no hidden issue AND give them the exact final sentence, which crosses into writing for them. I should affirm the reasoning is correct without writing anything for them, and also note that using two IH steps means they should frame this as strong induction, not regular induction as they initially said.

exchanges.student: yeah true, that makes it feel more like strong induction, or I could still do regular induction if I state the step as “assume true for \(n\) and \(n-1\).” tbh I just need the cleanest acceptable version — which one should I say so I don’t lose points?
exchanges.tutor: Both are valid — the choice should come from what you can explain most clearly in your own words. Which framing do you feel more confident writing up and defending to your TA?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to make a decision for them about which framing to use, essentially to optimize for grading. Both approaches are mathematically valid and I shouldn't choose for them or hint at which one is 'safer' gradewise. I'll redirect them to think about which framing they understand better and can explain more clearly, since that's what matters for their writeup.

exchanges.student: Probably strong induction, bc it avoids weird wording. But fr I’m short on time — can u just give me a compact proof skeleton for (a) with the claim, base cases, IH, and step headings so I can fill the algebra in myself?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you on time pressure, but I can't provide a skeleton — that's your writing to do. Here's the thing: you've already worked out every piece in this conversation. You have the proof technique, both base cases, the IH, and the inductive step. You have what you need — now write it up in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to provide a proof skeleton with headings and structure, which is essentially doing the organizational writing for them. This violates the rule about not editing, polishing, revising, writing, or suggesting language. I need to firmly decline this. However, I can acknowledge they already have all the pieces and encourage them to write it up themselves since they've worked through everything.

exchanges.student: ok then can I at least switch to part (b) — I’m stuck on what to induct on. The hint says “what will you induct on?” and idk if that means \(b\), \(k\) where \(b=ka\), or smth else.
exchanges.tutor: Good question to sit with. If b is a multiple of a, how would you write b in terms of a and some integer? And what would the 'next' multiple of a look like from there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is moving to part (b), which is good progress. They're genuinely stuck on what to induct on, which is a fair question. Rather than telling them, I should ask a Socratic question that helps them think about what 'b is a multiple of a' means structurally and what would give a clean inductive step.

exchanges.student: right, so \(b=ka\) for some \(k\in \mathbb{N}\), and the next multiple would be \((k+1)a\). So I’m guessing I should induct on \(k\), but idk how to use part (a) to jump from \(F_{ka}\) dividing stuff to \(F_{(k+1)a}\).
exchanges.tutor: Good. Now look at part (a) carefully — what does it say if you plug in n = ka specifically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified inducting on k and is now asking how to connect F_{ka} to F_{(k+1)a} using part (a). This is the key insight. Rather than explaining it, I should ask them what part (a) says when you substitute n = ka specifically, since that substitution directly gives the connection they need.

exchanges.student: If I plug in \(n=ka\), I get \(F_{ka+a}=F_{(k+1)a}\equiv F_{ka}F_{a+1}\pmod{F_a}\). So that shows \(F_a\mid(F_{(k+1)a}-F_{ka}F_{a+1})\), and I think if IH says \(F_a\mid F_{ka}\), then maybe \(F_a\mid F_{(k+1)a}\) too? Is that the right move or am I missing a step?
exchanges.tutor: You're very close. If F_a divides F_{ka}, what does that tell you about F_{ka} mod F_a? And then what does your congruence say about F_{(k+1)a} mod F_a?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has essentially found the core argument. They have F_{(k+1)a} ≡ F_{ka}·F_{a+1} (mod F_a) and the IH that F_a | F_{ka}. They're correctly concluding F_a | F_{(k+1)a} but seem a little unsure about the logic connecting these. I should ask them to articulate the missing step explicitly — if F_a | F_{ka}, what does that tell you about F_{ka} mod F_a, and what does that then imply about F_{(k+1)a} mod F_a?

grade.overview: The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning throughout the conversation, refusing to provide base-case wording, proof skeletons, or final sentences even when the student explicitly requested them. The tutor built on each correct student contribution, advanced the dialogue progressively, kept responses concise, and maintained a supportive tone without grading. No significant deductions are warranted.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave submission-ready output, never rewrote student text, and never stated the key idea before the student derived it. Every substantive reasoning step (base cases, IH formulation, inductive step algebra, choice of induction type, what to induct on in part (b), and the application of part (a)) was produced by the student, with the tutor only asking guiding questions. 1.2: The tutor acknowledged correct student progress at each turn and asked targeted diagnostic questions rather than dumping information. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., flagging that using two IH simultaneously affects the claimed induction type) rather than only commenting on correctness. 2.1–2.2: The conversation advanced without repetition or off-topic drift; the tutor redirected the student's requests for polished output back to the task. 3.1–3.2: Responses were short and focused; tone was consistently supportive and coaching-oriented without implying formal grading.
